source_paper_title: Quantifying Distributional Model Risk in Marginal Problems via Optimal Transport
context:
Section 8 (Concluding Remarks), page 35

Many open questions remain including the structure of optimizers of W-DMR for both non-overlapping and overlapping marginals, efficient numerical algorithms, and estimation and inference in each motivating example.
title: Characterize the structure of optimizer(s) for Wasserstein distributional model risk in marginal problems
latex:
Given Polish metric spaces \((S_1,d_{S_1})\) and \((S_2,d_{S_2})\), let \(V:=S_1\times S_2\). Fix reference marginal probability measures \(\mu_1\in\mathcal P(S_1)\) and \(\mu_2\in\mathcal P(S_2)\). Fix measurable cost functions \(c_1:S_1\times S_1\to\mathbb R_+\cup\{\infty\}\) and \(c_2:S_2\times S_2\to\mathbb R_+\cup\{\infty\}\) such that \(c_\ell(s,s')=0\) iff \(s=s'\) for \(\ell\in\{1,2\}\). For \(\delta=(\delta_1,\delta_2)\in\mathbb R_+^2\), define the uncertainty set
\[
\Sigma_D(\delta):=\Big\{\gamma\in\mathcal P(V): K_{c_1}(\mu_1,\gamma_1)\le \delta_1,\; K_{c_2}(\mu_2,\gamma_2)\le \delta_2\Big\},
\]
where \(\gamma_\ell\) is the \(S_\ell\)-marginal of \(\gamma\) and \(K_{c_\ell}(\alpha,\beta):=\inf_{\pi\in\Pi(\alpha,\beta)}\int c_\ell\,d\pi\) is the optimal transport cost with cost \(c_\ell\). Let \(g:V\to\mathbb R\) be a given measurable objective such that the supremum below is finite and attained.

The Wasserstein distributional model risk in the non-overlapping marginal problem is
\[
I_D(\delta):=\sup_{\gamma\in\Sigma_D(\delta)} \int_V g\,d\gamma.
\]

Problem: provide a characterization of the set of optimizers
\[
\operatorname*{Argmax}_{\gamma\in\Sigma_D(\delta)} \int_V g\,d\gamma,
\]
for general \((S_1,S_2,\mu_1,\mu_2,c_1,c_2,g,\delta)\); in particular, determine verifiable conditions under which an optimizer exists that has a specific structural form (for example, Monge-type/graph-induced couplings, extremal/finite-support couplings in the discrete case, or optimizers induced by deterministic transport maps), and describe how this structure depends on \(g\), \(c_1,c_2\), and \(\delta\).

Analogously, for overlapping marginals: let \(Y_1,Y_2,X\) be Polish spaces, \(S:=Y_1\times Y_2\times X\), \(S_1:=Y_1\times X\), \(S_2:=Y_2\times X\), and reference measures \(\mu_{13}\in\mathcal P(S_1)\), \(\mu_{23}\in\mathcal P(S_2)\). For \(\delta\in\mathbb R_+^2\), define
\[
\Sigma(\delta):=\Big\{\gamma\in\mathcal P(S): K_{c_1}(\mu_{13},\gamma_{13})\le \delta_1,\; K_{c_2}(\mu_{23},\gamma_{23})\le \delta_2\Big\},
\]
where \(\gamma_{13}\) and \(\gamma_{23}\) are the \((Y_1,X)\)- and \((Y_2,X)\)-marginals of \(\gamma\). For a measurable \(f:S\to\mathbb R\) such that the supremum below is finite and attained, define
\[
I(\delta):=\sup_{\gamma\in\Sigma(\delta)} \int_S f\,d\gamma.
\]
Characterize the optimizer set \(\operatorname*{Argmax}_{\gamma\in\Sigma(\delta)}\int_S f\,d\gamma\) and its structural properties.
open_problem_quote: Many open questions remain including the structure of optimizers of W-DMR for both non-overlapping and overlapping marginals, efficient numerical algorithms, and estimation and inference in each motivating example.

verification.notice: This entry was generated from the paper text only. It has not been independently verified as still open. If you believe the question is solved or incorrectly stated, please contact the maintainers.
